title: Teaching the Central Limit Theorem
text: like teach students Central Limit Theorem, simulating data great option. can show distribution single sample skewed data small sample size sampling distribution mean still skewed. can repeat medium sample size larger sample size can see shape sampling distribution changes even shapes individual samples remains . want students get even comfortable Central Limit Theorem, can students practice simulating data well. can change sample sizes, number samples drawn, distributional shape set samples see factors impacts sampling distribution. generate_distribution() function edsamplr great resource wrapper function simulating data variety distributional shapes. students don’t remember rnorm(), rbinom(), rgamma(), etc. can just name distribution first argument function. Adding scenario data simulated can also help students grasp concept fully provides additional “hook” students latch onto, aid remembering.

title: Teaching the Central Limit Theorem
text: Coconut College investigating student study habits, particularly many hours students spend preparing midterm exam introductory statistics. administrators conduct preliminary survey one section (12 students) class meets right outside administration offices.  revealed students study 1.724 hours average. Administrators concerned may low. seek support, note 12 students large representative sample since required course serves hundreds students year. remember Central Limit Theorem says sample size increases, sampling distribution mean becomes close normal, even underlying distribution skewed. Knowing amount study time must positive, continuous, likely right skewed, decide simulate sample data gamma distribution various sample sizes help make decision sample size .  start 12 since sample size preliminary survey simulate 1,000 samples.  feel affirmed original thought sample size 12 large enough. decide double sample size see sampling distribution mean looks like another 1,000 samples.  looks closer normal, feel confident Central Limit Theorem let . decide add another dozen sample size.  , , looks better 12, quite want . just recalled Central Limit Theorem rule thumb n=30, know underlying distribution won’t normal. add 2 dozen just see happens.  Since ’re just simulating, decide might well try 100.  Based simulations, feel renewed sense confidence decision rely Central Limit Theorem ready go survey students.
code: n <- 12 alpha <- 3  small_sample <- data.frame(Value = generate_distribution(distribution = "gamma",                                                          n = n,                                                          alpha_shape = alpha,                                                          summary = FALSE)) small_sample %>%   summarize(min = round(min(Value), 3),             q1 = round(quantile(Value, 0.25), 3),             median = round(median(Value), 3),             q3 = round(quantile(Value, 0.75), 3),             max = round(max(Value), 3),             mean = round(mean(Value), 3),             sd = round(sd(Value), 3)) #>     min    q1 median    q3   max  mean    sd #> 1 0.586 1.066   1.39 2.216 3.821 1.724 1.037  small_sample %>%   ggplot(aes(y = Value)) +   geom_boxplot() replications <- 1000  small_sample_list <- replicate(replications,                                expr = generate_distribution(distribution = "gamma",                                                      n = n,                                                      alpha_shape = alpha,                                                      summary = FALSE),                                simplify = FALSE)  small_sampling_dist <- data.frame(sample = seq(1:replications),                                   mean = sapply(small_sample_list, mean, na.rm = TRUE))   small_sampling_dist %>%   ggplot(aes(x = mean)) +   geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.05, alpha = 0.6, color = "black") n <- 24  medium_sample_list <- replicate(replications,                                expr = generate_distribution(distribution = "gamma",                                                      n = n,                                                      alpha_shape = alpha,                                                      summary = FALSE),                                simplify = FALSE)  medium_sampling_dist <- data.frame(sample = seq(1:replications),                                   mean = sapply(medium_sample_list, mean, na.rm = TRUE))   medium_sampling_dist %>%   ggplot(aes(x = mean)) +   geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.05, alpha = 0.6, color = "black") n <- 36  large_sample_list <- replicate(replications,                                expr = generate_distribution(distribution = "gamma",                                                      n = n,                                                      alpha_shape = alpha,                                                      summary = FALSE),                                simplify = FALSE)  large_sampling_dist <- data.frame(sample = seq(1:replications),                                   mean = sapply(large_sample_list, mean, na.rm = TRUE))   large_sampling_dist %>%   ggplot(aes(x = mean)) +   geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.05, alpha = 0.6, color = "black") n <- 60  extra_large_sample_list <- replicate(replications,                                      expr = generate_distribution(distribution = "gamma",                                                            n = n,                                                            alpha_shape = alpha,                                                            summary = FALSE),                                      simplify = FALSE)  extra_large_sampling_dist <- data.frame(sample = seq(1:replications),                                         mean = sapply(extra_large_sample_list, mean, na.rm = TRUE))   extra_large_sampling_dist %>%   ggplot(aes(x = mean)) +   geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.05, alpha = 0.6, color = "black") n <- 100  massive_sample_list <- replicate(replications,                                  expr = generate_distribution(distribution = "gamma",                                                        n = n,                                                        alpha_shape = alpha,                                                        summary = FALSE),                                  simplify = FALSE)  massive_sampling_dist <- data.frame(sample = seq(1:replications),                                     mean = sapply(massive_sample_list, mean, na.rm = TRUE))   massive_sampling_dist %>%   ggplot(aes(x = mean)) +   geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.05, alpha = 0.6, color = "black")

title: Simulating Data with Correlated Variables
text: Imagine teaching students working correlated data. want give students real-life example published research, article readily available involves interaction. decide going simulate data based one explanatory variables response variable instead searching different article.

title: Simulating Data with Correlated Variables
text: first objective study investigate association work-home interaction (process functioning one two domains positively negatively influenced domain) teachers’ life satisfaction. second objective test moderating role teachers’ core-self evaluations (self-esteem, self-efficacy, locus control, neuroticism) relationship work-home interaction life satisfaction. cross-sectional study conducted sample 168 Romanian teachers. Results indicated positive work-home interactions (situations aspects work facilitate functioning home vice versa) positively related life satisfaction teachers low core-self evaluations. Negative work-home interactions (situations aspects work interfere functioning home vice versa) negatively related teachers’ life satisfaction, core self-evaluations moderate relationships. Theoretical practical implications discussed. (Nastasa et al. 2020)

title: Simulating Data with Correlated Variables
text: Nastasa colleagues (2020) multiple research questions much complex design need class, looking descriptive statistics table (Table 1), decide focus self-evaluation life satisfaction.  Note: Cronbach’s alpha reliabilities parentheses diagonal, /p<.05, //p*<.01. (Nastasa et al. 2020)  Explanatory variable: Self-evaluation Response variable: Life satisfaction can use generate_correlated() function simulate data resemble . Since already created data frame selected variable information, can plug . variances variables, want make sure indicate use_slope = FALSE. default summary argument (TRUE), output provides list four elements: correlation simulated sample slope simulated sample summary statistics input “parameters” (statistics study) resulting sample sample data just want sample data, can turn summary just get data frame simulated data. ’re happy final product, ’re ready export data students. ’d like try get closer original data, can rerun function.
code: selected_vars <- data.frame(Variable = c("Self-Evaluation", "Life Satisfaction"),                             N = c(186, 186),                             Mean = c(4.08, 5.64),                             Var = c(0.48^2, 0.87^2),                             Cor = c(0.58, NA))  selected_vars #>            Variable   N Mean    Var  Cor #> 1   Self-Evaluation 186 4.08 0.2304 0.58 #> 2 Life Satisfaction 186 5.64 0.7569   NA output <- generate_correlated(n = selected_vars$N[1],                               mean = selected_vars$Mean,                               var = selected_vars$Var,                               r = selected_vars$Cor[1],                               use_slope = FALSE) data <- output$sample  output$correlation #> [1] 0.68 output$slope #> [1] 1.29 output$summary #>         mean   sd  skew kurtosis #> input 1 4.08 0.48  0.00     0.00 #> k 1     4.07 0.51 -0.13    -0.25 #> input 2 5.64 0.87  0.00     0.00 #> k 2     5.59 0.96  0.15     0.17

Unfortunately, find original data time contact authors. Fortunately, can use statistics article parameters simulating sample data.

title: Simulating Data with Proportions
text: Scopaesthesia, also known sense stared , widely reported supported controlled experiments showing detection rates significantly chance blindfolded subjects observed directly starers physically present. contrast, indirect observation remote starers CCTV demonstrated effects detectable physiologically changes electrodermal activity, reach conscious awareness. explore indirect scopaesthesia, tested whether people tell watched computer phone screens. Participants worked pairs. randomized trial, starer either saw live video staree, ‘distraction’ photograph. Starees reported felt stared . analysed results statistically overall hit rate, variables feedback, observer-subject relationship, distance. Across 6,050 trials, overall hit rate 49.9%, almost exactly 50% chance level. chance-level outcome affected feedback, gender starers starees, distance starers starees, multiple starers compared single starers. Thus, scopaesthesia undetectable consciously people stared indirectly via screens. (Sheldrake Penraeth 2024)

title: Simulating Data with Proportions
text: Sheldrake Penraeth (2024) much complex design need class, decide focus hit proportion ‘looking’ condition.  Explanatory variable: ‘Looking’ condition Response variable: Successful identification staring can use generate_categorical() function simulate data resemble . two variables two conditions , frequency table looks like: can turn proportion table. situation, want get sample data matches proportion table. may seem counter intuitive run generate_categorical() one variable 4 groups, trying simulate two separate categorical variables, rather 4 cross-conditions. like define arguments prior calling function can make sure everything lines . using separate argument, make sure put explanatory variable first, result variable. output provides simulated data descriptive summary comparing inputs simulated data. ’re happy final product, ’re ready export data students. ’d like try get closer original data, can rerun function.
code: staring_outcomes <- data.frame(Trials = c(2914, 2926),                                Hits = c(1654, 1255),                                Hit_Rate = c(56.8, 42.9),                                row.names = c("Seen", "Unseen"))  staring_outcomes #>        Trials Hits Hit_Rate #> Seen     2914 1654     56.8 #> Unseen   2926 1255     42.9 staring_counts <- data.frame(Hits = staring_outcomes$Hits,                              Misses = staring_outcomes$Trials - staring_outcomes$Hits,                              row.names = c("Seen", "Unseen"))  staring_counts #>        Hits Misses #> Seen   1654   1260 #> Unseen 1255   1671 staring_props <- round(staring_counts/sum(staring_counts), 3)  staring_props #>         Hits Misses #> Seen   0.283  0.216 #> Unseen 0.215  0.286 p <- 1 n <- 5840 k <- 4 k_prop <- c(0.283, 0.216,              0.215, 0.286) k_names <- c("Seen_Hit", "Seen_Miss",             "Unseen_Hit", "Unseen_Miss")  output <- generate_categorical(p = p,                                 n = n,                                 k = k,                                 k_prop = k_prop,                                k_names = k_names,                                separate = TRUE) data <- output$sample  output$proportion_summary #>           k    n   k1   k2 #> input p1  2 2922 0.28 0.22 #> sample p1 2 2922 0.50 0.50 #> input p2  2 2918 0.22 0.29 #> sample p2 2 2918 0.50 0.50

title: Teaching Independent Samples t Test
text: Contributing burgeoning area research nuanced effects emojis brand communications, current research builds understanding two dominant forms emoji role—emojis text reinforcement emojis text substitution—downstream effects. Across three studies, examine emoji roles differentially interact message features influence brand-level outcomes (brand attitudes, product quality expectations consumers’ willingness try brand’s product) effects processing fluency. find robust evidence substitution emojis elicit negative brand-level outcomes reinforcement emojis emoji low congruence text complexity text message high, effects mediated processing fluency. findings deepen understanding emojis’ effects brand communications provide practical guidance digital marketers regarding effectively leverage emojis. (Deng McShane 2025)

title: Teaching Independent Samples t Test
text: Deng McShane (2025) multiple studies complex design, first analysis first study independent samples tt test. authors compared processing fluency tweets contained emojis text just contained text found statistically significant difference two groups (Deng McShane 2025). Memoji=5.86 vs. Mtext=6.27,t(247)=2.73,p=0.003M_{emoji} = 5.86 \text{ vs. } M_{text} = 6.27, t(247) = 2.73, p = 0.003  Explanatory variable: Tweet type Response variable: Processing fluency  Since don’t variance standard deviation two groups, calculate . know FF tests later article sample sizes nemoji=171n_{emoji}=171 ntext=78n_{text}=78, can use results FF tests calculate variances. Now variances, can simulate data. generate_quantitative() function provides us sample data summary statistics comparing input output. can’t get exact match original data, can get close. Just check see, tt resulting data. able recreate findings, similar data. ’re happy final product, ’re ready export data students. ’d like try get closer original findings, can update arguments function re-run. Either way, make sure tell students original data sample simulated moments similar statistics original.
code: # what we know  n_emoji <- 171 n_text <- 78 m_emoji <- 5.86 m_text <- 6.27 t <- 2.73  # calculating variance from F stats  m_emoji_r <- 6 m_emoji_s <- 5.73 n_emoji_r <- 87 n_emoji_s <- 84 F_rs <- 2.15  diff_rs <- m_emoji_r - m_emoji_s var_emoji <- diff_rs^2/(F_rs * (1/n_emoji_r + 1/n_emoji_s))  F_st <- 9.65  se2_total <- (m_text - m_emoji_s)^2/F_st var_text <- (se2_total - (var_emoji/n_emoji_s)) * n_text sample <- generate_quantitative(k = 2,                               mean = c(m_emoji, m_text),                               var = c(var_emoji, var_text),                               n = c(n_emoji, n_text),                               k_names = c("emoji", "text"))  data <- sample$sample  sample$summary #>           n mean   sd  skew kurtosis #> input 1 171 5.86 1.20  0.00     0.00 #> k 1     171 5.96 1.17 -0.18     0.57 #> input 2  78 6.27 1.01  0.00     0.00 #> k 2      78 6.06 1.00  0.78     0.21 t.test(Value ~ Group, data = data, var.equal = TRUE) #>  #>  Two Sample t-test #>  #> data:  Value by Group #> t = -0.65978, df = 247, p-value = 0.51 #> alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group emoji and group text is not equal to 0 #> 95 percent confidence interval: #>  -0.4019664  0.2002403 #> sample estimates: #> mean in group emoji  mean in group text  #>            5.963175            6.064038

what: First Semester Statistics Course Data Simulator
title: First Semester Statistics Course Data Simulator
text: edsamplr package supports introductory statistics instruction functions generating sample data illustrate many topics first-semester statistics course. Users can specify parameters univariate bivariate distributions obtaining sample data. functions also calculate summary statistics illustrating sampling variation population parameters.

title: First Semester Statistics Course Data Simulator
text: can install development version edsamplr GitHub : source package, users must also install Rtools order install package. can install Rtools CRAN. can check see already Rtools installed pkgbuild: Note: code chunks, install.packages() line commented . either packages installed, need remove comment symbol (#) running code.
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("keahtandon/edsamplr") # install.packages("pkgbuild") pkgbuild::has_rtools(install_rtools = TRUE)

what: edsamplr: First Semester Statistics Course Data Simulator — edsamplr-package
title: edsamplr: First Semester Statistics Course Data Simulator — edsamplr-package
text: tool statistics educators generate simulated data major concepts first-semester statistics course. Functions include generating K groups/samples binary, categorical, quantitative data, matched pairs quantitative data specified parameters /moments. also function correlated quantitative data specified moments, slope, correlation coefficient. quantitative variables produced using Fleishman's power transformation method. provide parameters, provide data.

title: Generate samples of 2PL data — generate_2PL
text: generate_2PL() function generate items sample dichotomous response data n students.

title: Generate samples of 2PL data — generate_2PL
code: generate_2PL(   difficulty,   discrimination,   n,   identify_groups = FALSE,   group_pct = c(0.27, 0.27) )

title: Generate samples of 2PL data — generate_2PL
text: difficulty numeric vector difficulty values items. difficulty value provided item 0 1 scale. Values closer 0 indicate harder items, values closer 1 indicate easier items. discrimination numeric vector discrimination values items. discrimination value provided item -1 1 scale. Negative values extremely poorly discriminating. Values closer 0 indicate poorly discriminating item, values closer 1 indicate better discrimination. n numeric vector sample size. identify_groups logical vector whether students grouped low, medium, high achievement based simulated responses. group_pct numeric vector proportion students included high low groups. default 0.27. format c(high, low).

title: Generate samples of 2PL data — generate_2PL
text: data frame n rows columns (based length difficulty discrimination vectors). row represents vector scores (correct, incorrect) based student's response simulated test. column represents vector responses test item. simulated response values 0 incorrect response 1 correct response. items sorted increasing order difficulty. argument identify groups TRUE, total percentage correct column grouping column also return.

title: Generate samples of 2PL data — generate_2PL
text: function requires mirt statmod packages. already installed try run function, receive message installing . can install individually, run pak::pak(“keahtandon/edsamplr”, dependencies = TRUE) include install package. function originally designed generate samples dichotomous response data use teaching undergraduate education majors difficulty discrimination parameters two parameter logistic (2PL) model use reviewing selected-response tests making decisions based item parameters. 2PL model item-response theory model uses test response data model probability individual correctly responding test item based difficulty discrimination item given individual's underlying ability level (latent trait studied). Difficulty defined easy hard item calculated proportion respondents got item correct. Discrimination defined well item discriminates individuals high ability low ability. Items discriminate well higher probability correct answer high ability individuals low ability individuals. Users can select number items students generate responses based item-specific difficulty discrimination parameters. function simulates data based parameters. data simulated, sample similar exact parameters item. combinations difficulty discrimination parameters result viable output. occurs, error message display console indicating difficulty discrimination combination working provide suggested range change difficulty parameter . Update one parameters item re-run.

title: Generate samples of 2PL data — generate_2PL

what: Generate samples of binary data — generate_binary
title: Generate samples of binary data — generate_binary
text: generate_binary() function generate sample binary data.

title: Generate samples of binary data — generate_binary
code: generate_binary(   p = 1,   n = 100,   prop = 0.5,   group_names = c(1:0),   summary = TRUE,   replication = 1 )

title: Generate samples of binary data — generate_binary
text: p numeric vector number variables. default value 1. n numeric vector sample size. default value 100. prop numeric vector proportion successes. default value 0.5. k > 1, can provide separate proportion values k. group_names optional character vector naming successes failures. default values 1 success 0 failure. summary logical vector whether return include summary statistics. default value TRUE. replication numeric vector number times replicate sampling. default value 1.

title: Generate samples of binary data — generate_binary
text: summary = TRUE, list containing sample, data frame n rows k columns summary, matrix sample size, number successes, proportion statistic, proportion parameter variable, freq.table, matrix frequencies successes failures variable summary = FALSE, data frame n rows 1 column.

title: Generate samples of binary data — generate_binary
text: function generates samples binary data based specified proportions. can generate single column multiple columns data different proportions specified . data sampled specified proportions parameter, output statistics exact match parameters. output sample, summary argument allows summary statistics generated sample data. allows user easily compare simulation statistics specified parameters. useful introducing students basics probability, proportion tests, chi square tests without call two () separate rbinom() functions. interested using binary data sample student test responses, consider using generate_2PL(), simulates binary response data based specified difficulty discrimination parameters.

title: Generate samples of binary data — generate_binary

what: Generate samples of categorical data — generate_categorical
title: Generate samples of categorical data — generate_categorical

title: Generate samples of categorical data — generate_categorical

title: Generate samples of categorical data — generate_categorical
text: p numeric vector number variables. default value 1. n numeric vector sample size. Separate values n can entered unbalanced design. default value 100. k numeric vector number groups within variable. default value 1. p>1, can provide separate group numbers p. k_prop vector proportion group population. default value "equal". unequal proportions, provide concatenated vector length number groups (k). p>1, can provide separate sets proportions p. k_names optional character vector naming successes failures (group types). default values 1:k. want use separate argument, name groups underscores separating place split. separate logical vector indicating whether split groups separate columns simulating. works p = 1. default value FALSE. summary logical vector indicating whether return summary statistics. default value TRUE. replication numeric vector number times replicate sampling. default value 1.

text: list containing data frame ("sample") n rows p columns matrix ("proportion_summary") input sample proportions group variable.

title: Generate samples of categorical data — generate_categorical

what: Generate samples of correlated quantitative data — generate_correlated

title: Generate samples of correlated quantitative data — generate_correlated
code: generate_correlated(   n = 100,   mean = 0,   var = 1,   skew = 0,   kurt = 0,   slope = 1,   r = 0.5,   use_slope = TRUE,   summary = TRUE,   decimals = 3,   replication = 1 )

title: Generate samples of correlated quantitative data — generate_correlated
text: n numeric vector sample size. default value 100. mean numeric vector population mean first variable. default value 0. var numeric vector population variance first variable (variables want enter slope). default value 1. skew numeric vector population skew variables. Separate values skew can entered. default value 0. kurt numeric vector population kurtosis variables. Separate values kurtosis can entered. default value 0. slope optional numeric vector slope regression line. Either enter slope two variances. default value 1. r numeric vector correlation variables. default value 0.5. use_slope logical vector whether entering two variances one variance slope. default value TRUE. summary logical vector whether return include summary statistics. default value TRUE. decimals numeric vector number decimals round sample data . default value 3. replication numeric vector number times replicate sampling. default value 1.

title: Generate samples of correlated quantitative data — generate_correlated
text: summary = TRUE, list containing correlation sample, slope sample, data frame ("sample") n rows 2 columns, matrix ("summary") input parameters descriptive statistics. summary = FALSE, data frame n rows 2 columns.

title: Generate samples of correlated quantitative data — generate_correlated
text: function generates sample bivariate quantitative data based either specified slope specified correlation specified mean, variance, skew, kurtosis parameters. data sampled specified proportions parameter, output statistics exact match parameters. output sample, summary argument allows summary statistics generated sample data. allows user easily compare simulation statistics specified parameters. useful teaching scatterplots simple linear regression. replication argument useful teaching sampling distributions.

what: Generate sample data from named distributions — generate_distribution
title: Generate sample data from named distributions — generate_distribution
text: generate_distribution() wrapper function generating sample data named continuous distributions. distributional shapes include beta, Cauchy, chi-squared, exponential, F, gamma, Laplace, logistic, log normal, normal, t, triangular, uniform, Weibull. See additional details distribution parameters default values.

title: Generate sample data from named distributions — generate_distribution
code: generate_distribution(   distribution = c("beta", "cauchy", "chi-squared", "exponential", "f", "gamma",     "laplace", "logistic", "log normal", "normal", "t", "triangular", "uniform",     "weibull"),   n = 1000,   seed = NULL,   alpha_shape = NULL,   beta_shape = NULL,   location = NULL,   scale = NULL,   df = NULL,   df2 = NULL,   rate = NULL,   mean_log = NULL,   sd_log = NULL,   mean = NULL,   sd = NULL,   theta = NULL,   min = NULL,   max = NULL,   summary = TRUE )

title: Generate sample data from named distributions — generate_distribution
text: distribution character vector identify distributional shape sample. Options include beta, cauchy, chi-squared, exponential, f, gamma, laplace, logistic, log normal, normal, t, triangular, uniform, weibull. n numeric vector sample size. default value 1000. seed optional numeric vector use set.seed() alpha_shape non-negative numeric vector required shape beta, gamma, Weibull distributions. beta_shape non-negative numeric vector required shape beta distribution. location numeric vector location Cauchy, Laplace, logistic distributions. scale numeric vector scale Cauchy, Laplace, logistic, Weibull distributions. df numeric vector required degrees freedom chi-squared, F, t distributions. df2 numeric vector required degrees freedom F distribution. rate numeric vector rate exponential gamma distributions. mean_log numeric vector mean (log scale) log normal distribution. sd_log numeric vector standard deviation (log scale) log normal distribution. mean numeric vector mean normal distribution. sd numeric vector standard deviation normal distribution. theta numeric vector required theta triangular distribution. min numeric vector minimum value triangular uniform distributions. max numeric vector maximum value triangular uniform distributions. summary logical vector whether return include summary statistics. default value TRUE.

title: Generate sample data from named distributions — generate_distribution
text: list data, vector length n containing generated values summary, descriptive matrix input sample values specified distribution's parameters

title: Generate sample data from named distributions — generate_distribution
text: beta distribution requires alpha beta shape parameters. default values distribution. See stats::rbeta() information. Cauchy distribution uses location scale parameters. default values location = 0 scale = 1. See stats::rcauchy() information. chi-squared distribution requires df parameter. default value distribution. See stats::rchisq() information. exponential distribution uses rate parameter, default value 1. See stats::rexp() information. F distribution requires df df2 parameters. default values distribution. See stats::rf() information. gamma distribution requires alpha shape rate parameter. default value alpha shape, default value rate 1. See stats::rgamma() information. Laplace distribution uses location scale parameters. default values location = 0 scale = 1. See VGAM::rlaplace() information. logistic distribution uses location scale parameters. default values location = 0 scale = 1. See stats::rlogis() information. log normal distribution uses mean log sd log parameters. default values mean log = 0 sd log = 1. See stats::rlnorm() information. normal distribution uses mean sd parameters. default values mean = 0 sd = 1. See stats::rnorm() information. t distribution requires df parameter. default value distribution. See stats::rt() information. triangular distribution requires theta, min, max parameters. default value theta, default values min = 0 max = 1. See VGAM::rtriangle() information. uniform distribution uses min max parameters. default values min = 0 max = 1. See stats::runif() information. Weibull distribution requires alpha shape scale parameter. default value alpha shape, default value scale 1. See stats::rweibull() information.

title: Generate sample data from named distributions — generate_distribution

what: Generate samples of heteroscedastic data — generate_heteroscedastic
title: Generate samples of heteroscedastic data — generate_heteroscedastic
text: generate_heteroscedastic() function generate samples data specified degree heteroscedasticity

title: Generate samples of heteroscedastic data — generate_heteroscedastic
code: generate_heteroscedastic(n = 1000, degree)

title: Generate samples of heteroscedastic data — generate_heteroscedastic
text: n numeric vector sample size. default value 1000. degree numeric vector degree heteroscedasticity, 1 homoscedastic 5 extremely heteroscedastic.

title: Generate samples of heteroscedastic data — generate_heteroscedastic
text: data frame n rows 2 columns

title: Generate samples of heteroscedastic data — generate_heteroscedastic
text: function designed help teach students heteroscedasticity looks like. function allows degrees severity help show differences. useful students recently introduced may see nuance real data set. can also used data set demonstrating consequences violating equal variances assumption linear regression. ASA recommends use real data best practice, finding published data sets demonstrate heteroscedasticity challenging educational topics.

title: Generate samples of heteroscedastic data — generate_heteroscedastic
code: test <- generate_heteroscedastic(degree = 4) model <- lm(v2 ~ v1, data = test) plot(model, 2)   test <- generate_heteroscedastic(n = 20, degree = 2) model <- lm(v2 ~ v1, data = test) plot(model, 2)

what: Generate samples of quantitative matched pair data with correlation and optional standardized effect — generate_matched
title: Generate samples of quantitative matched pair data with correlation and optional standardized effect — generate_matched
text: generate_matched() function generate one-group samples quantitative matched pair data specified first four moments, correlation, optional standardized effect size

title: Generate samples of quantitative matched pair data with correlation and optional standardized effect — generate_matched
code: generate_matched(   n = 100,   mean = 0,   var = 1,   skew = 0,   kurt = 0,   effect_size = 0.5,   r = 0.5,   use_effect_size = TRUE,   summary = TRUE,   decimals = 3,   replication = 1 )

title: Generate samples of quantitative matched pair data with correlation and optional standardized effect — generate_matched
text: n numeric vector sample size. default value 100. mean numeric vector population mean first variable. default value 0. var numeric vector population variance first variable. default value 1. skew numeric vector population skew variables. Separate values skew can entered. default value 0. kurt numeric vector population kurtosis variables. Separate values kurtosis can entered. default value 0. effect_size optional numeric vector standardized effect size groups data. r numeric vector correlation variables. default value 0.5. use_effect_size logical vector indicate whether use standardized effect size generating data k k >= 2. default value FALSE. summary logical vector whether return include summary statistics. default value TRUE. decimals numeric vector number decimals round sample data . default value 3. replication numeric vector number times replicate sampling. default value 1.

title: Generate samples of quantitative matched pair data with correlation and optional standardized effect — generate_matched
text: summary = TRUE, list containing vector ("correlation") correlation pairs, matrix ("summary") input parameters descriptive statistics, data frame ("sample") n rows 3 columns (values set pair difference X2 X1). summary = FALSE, data frame n rows 3 columns.

title: Generate samples of quantitative matched pair data with correlation and optional standardized effect — generate_matched
text: function extension generate_quantitative() generates matched pair data based specified mean, variance, skew, kurtosis parameters. user can also specify standardized effect size difference two variables well correlation . data sampled specified proportions parameter, output statistics exact match parameters. output sample, summary argument allows summary statistics generated sample data. allows user easily compare simulation statistics specified parameters. useful teaching paired t tests, confounding variables, conditions valid inference.

Separate values n can entered unbalanced design. default value 100. effect_size optional numeric vector standardized effect size groups data. k_names optional character vector naming successes failures. default values 1:k. use_effect_size logical vector indicate whether use standardized effect size generating data k k >= 2. default value FALSE. summary logical vector whether return include summary statistics. default value TRUE. decimals numeric vector number decimals round sample data . default value 3. seed numeric vector use generating unbalanced distributions. default value 1234. replication numeric vector number times replicate sampling. default value 1.

what: Generate data using Headrick's fifth-order polynomial method — headrick_method
title: Generate data using Headrick's fifth-order polynomial method — headrick_method
text: headrick_method() uses specified values mean, variance, skew, kurtosis, fifth sixth moments determine constants expected values Y generate sample specified distribution.

title: Generate data using Headrick's fifth-order polynomial method — headrick_method
code: headrick_method(   k,   n,   mean,   var,   skew,   kurtosis,   corr = diag(1, k),   gam3 = NaN,   gam4 = NaN )

title: Generate data using Headrick's fifth-order polynomial method — headrick_method
text: k numeric vector indicating number variables sample. n numeric vector sample size. mean numeric vector length k means variable distribution. values can identical. var numeric vector length k variances variable distribution. values can identical. skew numeric vector length k skews variable distribution. values can identical. kurtosis numeric vector length k kurtoses variable distribution. values can identical. corr kxk correlation matrix. default value diag(1,k). gam3 optional numeric value 5th standardized cumulant. default value computed internally. gam4 optional numeric value 6th standardized cumulant. default value computed internally.

title: Generate data using Headrick's fifth-order polynomial method — headrick_method
text: data frame n rows k columns containing generated values.

title: Generate data using Headrick's fifth-order polynomial method — headrick_method
text: Headrick, T. C. (2002). Fast fifth-order polynomial transforms generating univariate multivariate nonnormal distributions. Computational Statistics & Data Analysis, 40, 685-711. Olvera Astivia, O. L., & Zumbo, B. D. (2015). Cautionary Note Use Vale Maurelli Method Generate Multivariate, Nonnormal Data Simulation Purposes. Educational Psychological Measurement, 75, 541-567.
